# Extracao: transcription_mp4_04__Finding_and_Working_with_a_Partner

- **Tipo:** TXT
- **Tamanho:** 0 MB

---

Finding your why is like panning for gold in the river of your past. You'll be recalling stories of your life and career from which some significant themes will emerge. Only you can retrieve those stories from your memory, but when it comes to interpreting them and finding the patterns that tie them together, a second set of eyes and ears, or a partner, is invaluable. To find a why, we need an objective perspective, which is something that has to come from someone else. You may be thinking, come on, I can do this myself, but please, trust us, we've guided thousands of people through this process. To discover and put clear language to your why, it is essential that you work with a partner. A partner can help us see our life experiences from a completely new perspective. Your partner should expect to give around three uninterrupted hours of their time to help you go through part two of the course. Ideally, you and your partner will meet in the same room, face to face. This ensures the best result, because they will be able to fully observe your emotion and body language as you tell your stories. If getting together with your partner in person just isn't possible, video or phone calls can work too. Okay, so David, walk us through what makes a great partner. Sure. Above all, we recommend that you choose someone you feel comfortable sharing personal information and feelings with. Even though it sounds counterintuitive, we urge you to avoid selecting a partner who knows you too well. In our experiences, spouses, close relatives, and best friends have a hard time being objective. They may be tempted to either tell your stories for you or correct the ones you tell. The best partners will be hearing your stories for the first time, and they will be someone who will listen and take notes as you recall your memories. Again, they should be someone who genuinely wants to help you discover your why. The best partners are innately curious, which makes them great at listening and great at asking follow-up questions. Someone trying to get to know you a little better will almost always ask more probing, unexpected, and thoughtful questions than someone who thinks they already know everything about you. On the next page, we'll provide some language which you can use to ask someone to be your partner. Feel free to give it your own personal twist. We've also included a link you can send them that provides some background on the why discovery process and some tips on how they can be the best partner possible. Feel free to visit that page yourself as well so you get a sense of what your partner's role is. If you need a little help deciding who to ask, write down the names of five people that you would say yes to if they asked you for the same favor. With that, you'll have a great short list of potential partners in hand.
